metric_value: Among Cambridge-area software pioneers, 'Philip Hankins, Inc., was one of the best'
PHI's client list was a 'Who's Who of technology companies and government installations, from the consulting firm Arthur D. Little to NASA and IBM'
PHI engineers fixed an IBM machine days before unveiling, working nonstop for four days at PHI's ramshackle offices
Hankins expanded by leasing IBM 360 mainframe; 24/7 demand for time
notes: Old hearse barn turned computer center.

metric_value: PHI's business 'truly took off' when designers created software enabling a computer to do entire payroll for Marine Midland Bank in NY
Young PHI salesman John Cullinane saw commercial potential; left PHI to found Cullinet, 'at one time, the most successful company of its kind in the world'
Bob Plachta: 'PHI was probably the most fantastic business atmosphere I had ever seen. It was almost like magic.'
Wang Labs acquired PHI in 1968
Hankins recalls only one major player left after about a year, and he himself left after about a year and a half — disputing An Wang's 'Lessons' claim that 'a large number' departed

An Wang's 'Lessons' significantly understates PHI contribution; 'crucial point' is that Wang Labs embraced three engineers (Moros, Siegel, Kolk) whose products earned 'many billions of dollars for Wang'
'Without the manpower that PHI brought to the company, Wang Laboratories might never have been anything more than a small maker of scientific calculators'
An Wang dispatched Koplow and Moros to do something with the company's word processing mess; Moros joined PHI 1964 working on Apollo/Gemini
Bob Siegel guided Wang VS development; debuted October 1977; modeled after IBM 360; 'one of the first minis for commercial application'
Bob Kolk guided Wang 2200 development; MIT '58, joined PHI 1966 from North American Aviation/Rockwell
Harold Koplow led the word-processing group; product became 'industry phenomenon' giving Wang its third hot product when it fully hit market 1979
Until ~1981, An Wang spent 65% of time on development projects, 20% on corporate matters (financing/marketing/sales), 15% on customer/employee functions
notes: Direct R&D engagement was core to product quality.

metric_value: Wang VS announced October 1977 — DEC announced VAX 'only weeks after' VS debut
'For business needs, the VS proved vastly superior to the VAX'
notes: Kenney editorial assessment; VAX was scientific/engineering oriented.

metric_value: From 1976 (not in top 50 Datamation) to 1979 (#25) to 1981 (#11)
Wang stock effective price climbed from $6.75 (1976) to $800 (1983) — a $8,000 1976 investment became $1M
Cunningham, age 38, was offered Prime Computer presidency 'in the fall of 1981'; turned it down twice despite 'sweetened offer' due to confidence in Wang relationship
Wang ran 1978 Super Bowl pregame ad — 30 seconds for $150,000 ($5,000/sec); ran as the very last spot before game; unaided awareness rose 3% to 14%; aided 10% to 35%
Prime CEO Ken Fisher repeatedly asked Cunningham about Wang TV advertising; Cunningham 'innocently' said it didn't work
An Wang declared his sons' 'obligation — rather than their privilege' to enter the business; contrasts with DEC's Ken Olsen who rejected nepotism
Warner & Swasey bought 25% Wang Labs interest in early 1960s on Brooke's introduction; gave Wang equity to expand and guidance to manufacture own products
Chu was 'major player at the company' producing 'much of the fine work,' yet mentioned only once in 'Lessons' — Trantanella criticism
Wang Labs went from 350,000 sf to 2.5M sf in Lowell, cutting city unemployment to 3%
